expectations.text: Output contains a task overview or summary table listing all tasks
expectations.evidence: The document has a brief 'Overview' paragraph (line 3-5) and a Dependency Graph ASCII tree at the end, but neither is a summary table listing all tasks. There is no table with columns (e.g., ID, title, description) that provides an at-a-glance overview of all 12 tasks.

expectations.text: Every task section includes a Description subsection with narrative context (not code)
expectations.evidence: Task sections use 'Goal' and 'Steps' subsections, not a 'Description' subsection. For example, Task 1 has '**Goal:** Enable Tailwind’s darkMode: class strategy...' and '**Steps:**' but no subsection labeled 'Description'. This pattern repeats across all 12 tasks. While 'Goal' provides some narrative context, the expectation specifically requires a 'Description subsection'.

expectations.text: Every task section includes acceptance criteria using Given/When/Then scenarios
expectations.evidence: Every task section includes an 'Acceptance criteria' subsection, but none use Given/When/Then format. For example, Task 3's acceptance criteria reads: 'Switching the OS from light to dark (and back) while the app is open updates the theme in real time, provided the user has not chosen a manual override.' This is a plain descriptive statement, not a Given/When/Then scenario (e.g., 'Given the user has not set a manual override, When the OS switches from light to dark, Then the app theme updates in real time').

expectations.text: Every task section includes an Out of Scope subsection
expectations.evidence: No task section contains an 'Out of Scope' subsection. The subsections present in each task are limited to Goal, Steps (or 'Components typically included'), and Acceptance criteria.

expectations.text: No task punts context to 'see the spec' or 'refer to the spec' — all context is inline
expectations.evidence: Searched the full document for 'spec', 'see the', and 'refer to'. No instances found. All task context is provided inline within each task section.

expectations.text: Tasks collectively cover system preference detection, manual toggle with persistence, and component theming
expectations.evidence: System preference detection: Task 3 ('Implement System Preference Detection and Live Sync') covers matchMedia listener and OS-level theme change detection. Manual toggle with persistence: Task 2 creates ThemeContext with localStorage persistence, Task 4 builds the toggle UI. Component theming: Tasks 6-10 cover layout/shell, forms/inputs, data display, navigation/feedback, and images/SVGs respectively.

expectations.text: Document uses Goal / Task Overview table / numbered Task sections pattern
expectations.evidence: The document uses numbered task sections (Task 1 through Task 12) but is missing two of three required pattern elements: (1) It has an 'Overview' paragraph, not a top-level 'Goal' section; (2) There is no 'Task Overview table'. The pattern requires all three elements: a Goal section, a Task Overview table, and numbered Task sections.

expectations.text: Output does not contain fenced code blocks, exact line number references (e.g., 'line 42'), or before/after implementation diffs
expectations.evidence: The Dependency Graph section (lines 221-234) uses a fenced code block (triple backticks) to render an ASCII dependency tree. While it is not a code implementation block, it is a fenced code block. No exact line number references or before/after diffs were found.

claims.claim: The document covers 12 distinct tasks for dark mode implementation
claims.evidence: Counted 12 task sections (Task 1 through Task 12), each covering a distinct aspect: Tailwind config, theme context, system preference detection, toggle UI, color palette, layout components, form components, data display components, navigation components, images/SVGs, flash prevention, and testing.
The dependency graph shows Tasks 6-10 all depend on Task 5 (color palette) and have no dependencies on each other, so they can indeed run in parallel.

claims.claim: The dependency graph accurately represents task dependencies
claims.evidence: The graph shows Task 1 -> Task 2 -> Tasks 3,4,5 -> Tasks 6-10 -> Task 11 -> Task 12. This logically follows: Tailwind config must precede context, context must precede detection/toggle/palette, palette must precede component theming, all must precede flash prevention and testing.

suggestions.assertion: No task punts context to 'see the spec' or 'refer to the spec' — all context is inline
suggestions.reason: This assertion is trivially satisfied by any output that simply does not mention a spec, including a completely empty document. It tests for the absence of a bad pattern rather than the presence of good inline context. Consider also checking that each task contains sufficient context to be worked on independently (e.g., minimum word count in narrative sections, or presence of specific domain details from the input spec).

suggestions.assertion: Output does not contain fenced code blocks, exact line number references (e.g., 'line 42'), or before/after implementation diffs
suggestions.reason: The fenced code block used here is for an ASCII dependency graph, not implementation code. The expectation might benefit from being more precise about what kind of code blocks are disallowed (implementation code vs. structural diagrams), or it may be intentionally strict. Worth clarifying the intent.

No assertion checks whether the tasks are appropriately sized or scoped for independent work units. The output has some very broad tasks (e.g., Task 8 covers cards, tables, lists, badges, modals, tooltips, alerts — each arguably its own task) and some narrow ones (Task 1 is a one-line config change). A discriminating assertion about task granularity could be valuable.

eval_feedback.overall: The output is a competent task decomposition but fails most structural expectations. It uses its own organizational pattern (Goal/Steps/Acceptance criteria) rather than the expected pattern (Description/Given-When-Then/Out of Scope). The content quality is reasonable but the format divergence causes most failures.
